clean_story: Empty space that isn’t.  Energy hidden in nothing. "Void physics" studies the strange rules of what we call "empty" space—because even a perfect vacuum hums with invisible activity. Picture a room with every atom removed. —Now imagine the walls still vibrating from ghostly pairs of particles popping in and out of existence. This is the quantum vacuum: not a dead void, but a bubbling broth of borrowed energy. Particles like electrons and positrons appear for fractions of a second—like thieves stealing energy from the universe, then returning it before getting caught. We’ve measured this. The Casimir effect proves it:Two metal plates placed micrometers apart in a vacuum pull toward each other—pushed by the pressure of these fleeting particles outside the gap. Black holes exploit void physics too. Hawking radiation shows they leak energy as particle pairs tear apart at their edges—one swallowed, one escaping as heat. Three related subjects are dark energy, quantum foam, and false vacuums.
